summary: Spolsky analyzes the browser landscape in mid-2003, noting Microsoft's decision to stop developing Internet Explorer as a standalone product after winning the browser war. He celebrates Mozilla Firebird as the first browser to genuinely catch up with IE, making it his new default browser. He argues that real competition could force Microsoft to improve IE again, potentially creating a two-browser world. He also dissects the AOL-Microsoft settlement, pointing out the irony that AOL owns Netscape but still depends on IE's component architecture because Gecko is too hard to embed, and that only one employee is working on fixing this problem.
key_points: Microsoft stopped investing in IE after reaching 90% market share, treating the browser war as won
Mozilla Firebird is the first browser in three years of Mozilla releases that genuinely rivals IE in usability and adds compelling new features like tabbed browsing and incremental search
Mozilla and Opera's progress was delayed by ill-advised ground-up rewrites, giving IE time to dominate
AOL paid $4.2 billion for Netscape but can't use Gecko because it lacks IE's easy COM embedding interfaces, making the acquisition nearly useless
Only one Netscape employee is working—in his spare time—on the COM compatibility layer that could make Gecko actually useful to AOL
money_quotes: There has been virtually no work on the IE web browser for a couple of years now and it looks like Microsoft has no interest in spending resources on a battle they already feel they've won.
Meanwhile the Mozilla and Opera teams were getting bogged down in ground-up rewrites, oops, so there was little or no serious competition.
After downloading virtually every Mozilla release over the last three years, this is the first browser I'm actually going to make my default web browser.
Now with a good code base to build upon, Firebird is likely to soar past IE in functionality and performance.
Considering that AOL spent $4.2 billion to buy Netscape, you'd think somebody would have noticed that they already have a browser component.

The Internet Explorer component is so much easier to embed in applications than Gecko that it probably comes down to the programmers on the AOL client team who just don't want to undergo the pain to embed Gecko.
This poor sod Adam Lock is working in his spare time to save all your jobs. Wake up.
key_insight: When a monopolist stops innovating after winning, the opening for competitors is real—but only if those competitors avoid architectural mistakes like ground-up rewrites and actually solve the practical integration problems that keep users locked in.
